channel.title: Feedly Digest
channel.description: Current article list from the Feedly digest on maraz.sk.

item.title: [Engineering] TBM 425: AI and Agency
item.description:
Cutler argues that AI adoption mandates often destroy the agency they claim to promote: teams need room to test, reject, and adapt tools against real problems, not perform adoption for executive metrics.

  • AI adoption is not just a training problem; trust, autonomy, and local context determine whether people use the tools well.
  • Skepticism is part of agency, especially when teams are being asked to change craft practices under management pressure.
  • Useful AI rollout starts from real work and evidence, not quota-style proof that people are prompting enough.

item.title: [Frontend] How's Linear so fast? A technical breakdown
item.description:
Brotzky breaks down Linear's perceived speed as an architecture choice: IndexedDB-backed local state, optimistic mutations, background sync, small modern-browser bundles, precaching, and animations that avoid blocking the workflow.

  • The browser-side database removes network latency from the core interaction loop; sync becomes a background concern.
  • Fast product feel is cumulative: local-first data, chunking, service-worker precache, GPU-friendly animation, and keyboard-first flows reinforce each other.
  • The tradeoff is architectural commitment; Linear started with a sync engine because that foundation is hard to retrofit later.

item.title: [Frontend] Building a predictive text input in React
item.description:
Huggins shows why inline autocomplete is awkward in React: mixed plain and ghost text pushes the implementation from standard inputs into contentEditable, cursor management, direct DOM mutation, and update-loop guards.

  • Native inputs are too limited when a single editing surface needs both accepted text and styled prediction text.
  • React's declarative model can be the wrong abstraction at the cursor boundary; refs and controlled mutation flags become practical tools.
  • The hard parts are selection preservation, programmatic text replacement, and preventing internal DOM edits from re-triggering React updates.

item.title: [Frontend] React server components in TanStack
item.description:
Rackis explains TanStack Start's explicit RSC model: server-only component trees and server functions can remove browser JavaScript when the tree shape or dependencies justify the split.

  • RSC is most useful when it prevents large server-only dependencies or component subtrees from entering the client bundle.
  • Explicit server functions make the server/client boundary easier to reason about than implicit framework magic.
  • The benefit is workload-dependent; simple client-heavy screens may gain little from the extra model.

item.title: [Frontend] One core, six frameworks, zero runtime abstraction
item.description:
Hiller describes a cross-framework form library that shares core logic while compiling to each framework's native reactivity instead of shipping a runtime adapter layer.

  • A cross-framework library does not have to collapse everything into a lowest-common-denominator abstraction.
  • Build-time specialization preserves native signals, refs, state cells, batching, and scheduling semantics.
  • Shared core logic gives multi-framework leverage, while framework-specific reactivity keeps runtime overhead low.

item.title: [Frontend] The best loading states are no loading states
item.description:
Smith argues that teams should spend less effort polishing spinners and skeletons and more effort making navigation land on finished pages through preloading, route-level data work, and slow-path fixes.

  • Loading UI is often a symptom of latency that should be hidden or removed, not a destination for more design polish.
  • Hover, viewport, and route-level prefetch can move waiting time before the user commits to navigation.
  • Persistent loading states are performance feedback; fix the slow path instead of normalizing the wait.

item.title: [Programming] Jira IS Turing-Complete
item.description:
Seriot turns the old Jira automation joke into a concrete reduction: linked issue counts act as registers, Epic status acts as the program counter, and automation rules implement Minsky-machine branching.

  • The proof includes a concrete setup and execution trace, not just the folklore claim.
  • Workflow state, issue creation/deletion, and conditional JQL rules are enough to model increment, decrement, branch, and halt.
  • The exercise is a useful reminder that sufficiently flexible automation systems become programming languages whether or not they are designed that way.

item.title: [Programming] Every byte matters
item.description:
Zakaria uses cache sizes and access patterns to show that object layout and byte count can dominate performance once memory bandwidth and cache locality are the bottleneck.

  • Asymptotic complexity is not enough when the working set no longer fits the relevant cache level.
  • Array-of-structs and struct-of-arrays choices depend on how the program actually reads fields.
  • Smaller structures reduce bandwidth pressure and can beat cleaner-looking layouts when access patterns are hot.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Reverse engineering the Creative Katana V2X soundbar to be able to control it from Linux
item.description:
The write-up documents a disciplined hardware/protocol reverse-engineering path: inspect the vendor app, capture USB traffic operation by operation, infer the serial framing, and build Linux control tooling from reproducible packet evidence.

  • Separate captures per UI operation turn opaque proprietary behavior into a tractable protocol map.
  • Firmware update traffic can expose payload format and command structure when ordinary controls are still unclear.
  • The method matters more than the soundbar: isolate the transport, record known actions, infer framing, then implement the minimum useful control surface.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] As an AI cautionist, I wish some of my coworkers would at least use it as a basic sanity check
item.description:
The thread surfaces a practical middle position on AI in engineering: even skeptics can use models as cheap review assistants for obvious mistakes, leakage, bad payload paths, and ignored error logs.

  • The useful baseline is not autonomous coding; it is catching defects a careful reviewer would catch when humans are moving too fast.
  • AI review adds value when paired with engineering judgment and concrete diffs, not when treated as authority.
  • The examples point to process failure: teams that will not inspect logs or test assumptions need stronger review habits, with or without AI.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Allowing LLM's to work fully autonomously is only viable when you have a process that automatically verifies it
item.description:
The post argues from a legacy-porting case that agentic coding only becomes viable when every change is constrained by an independent verifier, such as deterministic output diffs between old and new systems.

  • The hard part of AI coding is not generating code; it is proving that behavior stayed correct.
  • A good autonomy loop gives the model a task, runs it against a verifier, feeds back the concrete mismatch, and repeats.
  • Verification has to be external to the model's own explanation, especially for legacy migrations where plausible code can still be wrong.
